attrs.alt: Azure RBAC vs Microsoft Entra Roles: separate systems governing different layers

content.text: Azure has two independent role systems that are often confused because both use the word 'role'. They govern completely different layers of the platform and are managed via different portals, APIs, and PowerShell modules.

Azure RBAC: controls access to Azure resources (subscriptions, resource groups, VMs, storage accounts, Key Vaults, etc.). Roles are assigned via the IAM blade in the Azure portal and managed through the Az PowerShell module or az CLI.

Microsoft Entra Roles (formerly Azure AD roles): controls access to Entra ID directory objects (users, groups, applications, service principals, devices). Roles are assigned in the Entra ID portal and managed through Microsoft Graph or the AzureAD/Microsoft.Graph PowerShell modules.
A Global Administrator in Entra ID has no automatic access to Azure resources. A Subscription Owner in Azure RBAC has no automatic ability to manage directory objects.
Both systems independently use the term 'scope', 'role definition', and 'role assignment' — but the data lives in different stores and is evaluated by different engines.

The most common mistake: assuming Global Administrator grants Azure resource access, or that Subscription Owner grants the ability to manage users. Neither is true. They are completely separate systems.
Azure portal → IAM blade on a resource
Entra ID portal → Roles and administrators
Yes (custom directory roles with MS Graph permissions)
Tenant-wide by default; AU limits scope
Managing Entra Roles with Microsoft Graph

Entra roles use the Microsoft Graph API. The Az PowerShell module has no commands for Entra role management — you need either the Microsoft.Graph module, the Azure AD portal, or direct Graph API calls.

# Entra ID roles are managed via Microsoft Graph, not the Az PowerShell module.
# Install: Install-Module Microsoft.Graph -Scope CurrentUser
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'RoleManagement.Read.Directory'

# List all built-in Entra ID role definitions
Get-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleDefinition | Select-Object DisplayName, Id, IsBuiltIn |
    Sort-Object DisplayName

# List current role assignments (who has what Entra role)
Get-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleAssignment -ExpandProperty 'Principal,RoleDefinition' |
    Select-Object @{n='User';e={$_.Principal.AdditionalProperties.userPrincipalName}},
                  @{n='Role';e={$_.RoleDefinition.DisplayName}},
                  DirectoryScopeId

# Assign an Entra role to a user
$roleId = (Get-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleDefinition -Filter "displayName eq 'User Administrator'").Id
$userId = (Get-MgUser -UserId 'alice@contoso.com').Id
New-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleAssignment -RoleDefinitionId $roleId -PrincipalId $userId -DirectoryScopeId '/'
The deprecated AzureAD PowerShell module (Get-AzureADDirectoryRole) still works but is scheduled for removal. Migrate to Microsoft.Graph equivalents now.

Global Administrators can grant themselves User Access Administrator at the root management group — an Azure RBAC role, not an Entra role. This is designed as an emergency break-glass capability to restore access when no Azure RBAC administrators remain. It must be used with extreme care and immediately revoked after use.

# Global Administrator can ELEVATE to User Access Administrator at the root management group.
# This is a one-time break-glass capability — it creates an Azure RBAC assignment,
# NOT an Entra role assignment.

# Enable elevation (requires Global Administrator in Entra ID)
# This is done in the Entra ID portal:
# Azure Active Directory → Properties → Access management for Azure resources → Yes

# After enabling, the GA has User Access Administrator at:
# /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<root-tenant-mg-id>

# Verify the assignment was created:
$rootMG = (Get-AzManagementGroup | Where-Object { $_.TenantId -eq (Get-AzContext).Tenant.Id -and $_.Name -eq (Get-AzContext).Tenant.Id }).Id
Get-AzRoleAssignment -Scope $rootMG | Where-Object { $_.RoleDefinitionName -eq 'User Access Administrator' }

# IMPORTANT: Remove this elevated assignment immediately after completing the task.
# An open-ended User Access Administrator at root MG is a severe security risk.
Remove-AzRoleAssignment `
    -ObjectId          (Get-AzADUser -UserPrincipalName 'admin@contoso.com').Id `
    -RoleDefinitionName 'User Access Administrator' `
    -Scope             $rootMG
Enabling this creates an Azure RBAC assignment (visible in Get-AzRoleAssignment), not an Entra role assignment.
User Access Administrator at root MG grants the ability to manage all role assignments in the entire tenant — effectively as powerful as Subscription Owner across every subscription.
This setting is tenant-wide and persists until explicitly disabled. Check Entra ID → Properties → Access management for Azure resources regularly to confirm it is off.
Privileged Identity Management (PIM) cannot control this specific elevation — it is outside the standard PIM scope.
A common architecture mistake is granting one role and expecting it to provide access in both systems. Every persona that needs to work in both layers needs separate assignments in each system.

# The Global Administrator Entra role does NOT automatically grant Azure RBAC permissions.
# A Global Administrator cannot read Azure resources unless they also have an Azure RBAC role.

# Test: try listing VMs as a Global Admin who has no Azure RBAC role
# Get-AzVM will return nothing (or error) if no Reader or higher role is assigned.

# Correct pattern: assign both Entra role (for directory tasks) AND Azure RBAC (for resource tasks)
# Example: an identity administrator needs to:
#   - Manage user accounts → needs User Administrator (Entra role)
#   - Read Azure Advisor recommendations → needs Reader (Azure RBAC) at subscription scope

# Assign Entra role
$roleId = (Get-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleDefinition -Filter "displayName eq 'User Administrator'").Id
$userId = (Get-MgUser -UserId 'idadmin@contoso.com').Id
New-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleAssignment -RoleDefinitionId $roleId -PrincipalId $userId -DirectoryScopeId '/'

# Separately assign Azure RBAC role
New-AzRoleAssignment `
    -ObjectId          $userId `
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
    -Scope             '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
Manage users + read Azure Advisor recommendations
Read Entra sign-in logs + read Defender for Cloud alerts
Deploy Azure resources; no directory object access needed
None (unless the app uses Azure resources)

Privileged Identity Management supports just-in-time activation for both Azure RBAC roles and Entra roles, but they are configured and activated independently. Activating an eligible Azure RBAC role does not activate any Entra role, and vice versa.

# PIM manages BOTH Azure RBAC and Entra roles, but through different APIs.

# For Entra roles: use the Identity Governance PIM for Entra roles API
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'RoleManagement.ReadWrite.Directory','PrivilegedAccess.ReadWrite.AzureADGroup'

# List Entra PIM eligible role assignments
Get-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleEligibilityScheduleInstance |
    Select-Object PrincipalId, RoleDefinitionId, StartDateTime, EndDateTime

# For Azure RBAC: PIM uses the Azure Resource Manager API
# (az rest or Get-AzRoleEligibilityScheduleInstance)
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleEligibilityScheduleInstances?api-version=2020-10-01'

# Key difference: Entra role PIM activation happens in Entra ID and does NOT affect
# Azure RBAC evaluation. Azure RBAC PIM activation happens in ARM and does NOT affect
# Entra directory permissions. They are separate PIM scopes.

Administrative Units (AUs) provide a scoping mechanism for Entra roles analogous to resource groups in Azure RBAC. An AU contains a subset of directory objects (users, groups, or devices), and an Entra role assignment scoped to an AU only grants permissions over objects in that AU.

# Administrative Units (AUs) scope Entra roles to a subset of the directory.
# Example: a regional IT admin who should only manage users in the EMEA AU.

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'AdministrativeUnit.ReadWrite.All','RoleManagement.ReadWrite.Directory'

# Create an Administrative Unit
$au = New-MgAdministrativeUnit -DisplayName 'EMEA-Users' -Description 'Users in the EMEA region'

# Add users to the AU
$userId = (Get-MgUser -UserId 'user@contoso.com').Id
New-MgAdministrativeUnitMember -AdministrativeUnitId $au.Id -OdataId "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/$userId"

# Assign User Administrator scoped to the EMEA AU only
$roleId = (Get-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleDefinition -Filter "displayName eq 'User Administrator'").Id
$adminId = (Get-MgUser -UserId 'emea-admin@contoso.com').Id
New-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleAssignment `
    -RoleDefinitionId $roleId `
    -PrincipalId $adminId `
    -DirectoryScopeId "/administrativeUnits/$($au.Id)"

# The EMEA admin can now manage users in the EMEA AU but cannot touch
# users outside the AU or any Azure resources.
# Azure RBAC has no equivalent concept — it uses resource hierarchy, not AUs.
Scope role assignments to a resource subset
Scope role assignments to a directory object subset
Users, groups, or devices within the AU
No inheritance — AU is a flat container
Dev team has Contributor on dev-rg only
EMEA admin has User Administrator on EMEA users only

A complete access review must cover both Azure RBAC and Entra roles. The highest-risk identities are those with significant privileges in both systems simultaneously, as they can affect both cloud resources and identity infrastructure.
Global Administrator + Subscription Owner is the most dangerous combination. Treat any user holding both as an emergency review item.

Entra role assignments do not appear in Azure RBAC audit logs (Monitor / Activity Log). Entra role assignments are audited in the Entra ID Audit Log (graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/directoryAudits).
Microsoft Entra ID Governance access reviews can review both Entra roles and Azure RBAC role assignments from a single policy, simplifying periodic access certification.

Defender for Cloud's 'Identity' recommendations includes both 'MFA should be enabled on accounts with owner permissions on subscriptions' (Azure RBAC) and 'Global administrator role should not be assigned to more than 5 users' (Entra role).
Global Admin can manage all Azure resources
Global Admin has no Azure RBAC by default — Get-AzVM returns nothing
Assign Subscription Owner or specific RBAC roles separately
Removing Azure RBAC Owner also removes Entra access
Entra roles are unaffected by RBAC changes
Revoke Entra roles separately via Graph/Entra portal
User Administrator can reset any user's password
User Administrator cannot reset passwords of other admins unless scoped correctly
Check AU scoping and role exclusions
Service principal with Contributor can create app registrations
Contributor is an Azure RBAC role — app registrations need Entra Application Administrator or Application.ReadWrite.All Graph permission
Assign the Entra role or Graph API permission to the SP
PIM activation for Azure RBAC also activates Entra roles
They are separate PIM scopes — activation is independent
Activate each role separately in their respective PIM sections

Just as Azure RBAC supports custom role definitions, Entra ID supports custom directory roles. Custom Entra roles use microsoft.directory/* permission strings — a completely different namespace from Azure RBAC Actions.

# Create a custom Entra role (requires Entra ID P1 or P2 license)
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'RoleManagement.ReadWrite.Directory'

# Define the custom role with specific MS Graph permissions
$customRole = @{
    displayName   = 'Application Registration Auditor'
    description   = 'Can read all app registrations and service principals, cannot modify'
    isEnabled     = $true
    templateId    = [System.Guid]::NewGuid().ToString()
    rolePermissions = @(
        @{
            allowedResourceActions = @(
                'microsoft.directory/applications/standard/read'
                'microsoft.directory/servicePrincipals/standard/read'
                'microsoft.directory/applications/owners/read'
                'microsoft.directory/applications/credentials/standard/read'
            )
        }
    )
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5

# Create the role definition in Entra ID
$roleDef = New-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleDefinition -BodyParameter ($customRole | ConvertFrom-Json)
Write-Host "Created custom role: $($roleDef.Id)"

# Assign the custom role to a user (tenant-wide scope)
$userId = (Get-MgUser -UserId 'auditor@contoso.com').Id
New-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleAssignment `
    -RoleDefinitionId $roleDef.Id `
    -PrincipalId $userId `
    -DirectoryScopeId '/'

# Custom Entra roles use 'microsoft.directory/*' permissions — NOT the same
# as Azure RBAC Actions which use 'Microsoft.*/*/action' patterns.
Custom Entra roles require Entra ID P1 or P2 license (included in Microsoft 365 E3/E5).
Permission strings for Entra custom roles follow the pattern microsoft.directory/<object>/<property>/<action> — for example microsoft.directory/users/password/update.
Custom Entra roles can be scoped tenant-wide or to an Administrative Unit, but NOT to a specific user or group (unlike Azure RBAC which can scope to a resource).
The full list of allowable Entra custom role permissions is documented in Microsoft's 'Microsoft Entra built-in roles' reference under 'Custom role permissions'.
Service Principals and Entra Roles vs Graph API Permissions

Automation workloads (managed identities, service principals) often need to read Entra directory data. There are two approaches: assign an Entra directory role, or grant Microsoft Graph application permissions. The Graph API permission approach is generally preferred because it is more granular and explicit.

# Service principals can also hold Entra roles (for app-to-app access)
# Example: a DevOps pipeline that needs to read Entra group memberships
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All','RoleManagement.ReadWrite.Directory'

# Get the service principal for the pipeline's managed identity
$sp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "displayName eq 'devops-pipeline-mi'"

# Option 1: Assign an Entra role (e.g., Directory Reader) to the SP
$roleId = (Get-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleDefinition -Filter "displayName eq 'Directory Readers'").Id
New-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleAssignment `
    -RoleDefinitionId $roleId `
    -PrincipalId $sp.Id `
    -DirectoryScopeId '/'

# Option 2 (preferred for automation): Assign MS Graph API app roles instead of directory roles
# App roles (application permissions) are more granular and don't elevate the SP
# to a directory role that appears in the Entra roles assignment list.
$graphSP = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000098'"
$groupReadRole = ($graphSP.AppRoles | Where-Object { $_.Value -eq 'Group.Read.All' })
New-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignment `
    -ServicePrincipalId $sp.Id `
    -PrincipalId $sp.Id `
    -ResourceId $graphSP.Id `
    -AppRoleId $groupReadRole.Id

# This grants Group.Read.All to the managed identity without assigning any
# directory role — preferred for least-privilege automation scenarios.
Role assignment in Entra portal or via MgRoleManagement
Appears in Roles and administrators blade
App role assignment via Entra portal or API

Prefer Microsoft Graph application permissions over Entra directory roles for service principals. They follow the principle of least privilege better — you grant exactly the Graph operations needed rather than all the permissions bundled in a directory role.
Infrastructure as Code: Managing Both Systems

Terraform uses two separate providers for the two role systems: azuread for Entra roles and azurerm for Azure RBAC. Both can reference the same principal object ID, but the assignments live in different systems and have different lifecycle behavior.

The two role systems write to completely separate audit logs. Missing either source gives an incomplete picture of privileged access changes. Effective governance requires querying both and ideally streaming both to a common SIEM or Log Analytics workspace.

# Entra sign-in logs capture authentication events and Entra role context.
# Azure Activity Log captures ARM operations and Azure RBAC context.
# You need BOTH for a complete picture of privileged activity.

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'AuditLog.Read.All'

# Query Entra audit log for role assignment changes in the last 7 days
$start = (Get-Date).AddDays(-7).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')
Get-MgAuditLogDirectoryAudit `
    -Filter "activityDisplayName eq 'Add member to role' and activityDateTime ge $start" |
    Select-Object ActivityDateTime, InitiatedBy, TargetResources, Result

# Query Azure Activity Log for RBAC assignment changes
Get-AzActivityLog -StartTime (Get-Date).AddDays(-7) | Where-Object {
    $_.Authorization.Action -like '*/roleAssignments/*'
} | Select-Object EventTimestamp, Caller, @{n='Action';e={$_.Authorization.Action}}, @{n='Scope';e={$_.Authorization.Scope}}

# For unified SIEM integration, stream both to a Log Analytics Workspace:
# - Entra: Diagnostic Settings → Audit Logs → Log Analytics
# - Azure Activity: Activity Log → Diagnostic Settings → Log Analytics
# Then query both in KQL from a single workspace.
Entra role assignments, user changes, group changes, app consent
Entra ID portal → Monitoring → Audit logs
30 days (P1/P2: 30 days; extend via Log Analytics)
Entra ID portal → Monitoring → Sign-in logs
ARM operations including Azure RBAC changes, resource create/delete
Azure RBAC and Microsoft Entra roles are entirely separate systems with separate stores, APIs, portals, and PowerShell modules.
Azure RBAC governs Azure resource access; Entra roles govern directory object access. Neither crosses the boundary automatically.
Global Administrator is the highest Entra role but grants zero Azure resource access without a separate RBAC assignment.
Personas that need both types of access require separate assignments in each system.
Administrative Units scope Entra roles to a subset of directory objects, analogous to resource groups in Azure RBAC.
PIM supports both systems independently — activating one does not activate the other.
Cross-system audit (users with high Entra roles AND high Azure RBAC roles) is the most important combined governance check.
